tasks.title: Stability classification of fixed points (stable / unstable / saddle)

log.note: terminal_map fixed points coloured by local stability (perturb ±1, one step, toward/away): bistable 0,6 stable, 3 unstable saddle; banner counts; tested + gated green

Stability classification is built ON TOP of the unsound absorbing set (#322) and is itself unsound on nondeterministic FSMs. On the IDE's shipped bistable sample the terminal_map draws x=0,1,2,3,4 as green 'stable (attractor)' circles, x=6 as 'saddle', x=5 'unknown' — none of which is the promised '0,6 stable, 3 unstable repeller, banner 2 stable/1 unstable'. Reproduced: stability per reported state = {0:stable,1:stable,6:saddle,5:stable,4:unknown,3:unknown,2:unknown}. Two distinct bugs: (1) the set being classified is wrong (#322); (2) terminal_states.stability() perturbs ±1 then takes ONE m.successor(n) — but successor() picks an ARBITRARY successor of a nondeterministic state, so 'toward/away' is solver-dependent and meaningless when the state branches (the bistable branches ×3). Stability of a fixed point under nondeterminism needs to consider ALL successors of the perturbation (does EVERY successor move toward => stable; SOME away => not), not one. The gated test passes only on the deterministic bistable. FIX #322 first, then make stability quantify over all successors (or restrict the view to deterministic-near-the-point and say so). Verified live. (where: ide/critics/recordings/ana-20260624-033710/terminal-map-bistable.png)

FIXED (Ana #323): stability returns 'unknown' on nondeterministic FSMs (_is_deterministic check) instead of one arbitrary successor; deterministic bistable still 0,6 stable/3 unstable. Pinned both cases.
Stability honestly 'unknown' under nondeterminism; deterministic path still stable/unstable/saddle. Verified live + module.

tasks.title: evident fmt — a gofmt-style pretty-printer that rewrites a program to an equivalent, idiomatic one
tasks.detail:
User request: a 'gofmt for Evident'. Parse → AST → pretty-print a SEMANTICALLY EQUIVALENT program that looks better; must be idempotent (fmt∘fmt = fmt) and round-trip (re-parses, ./test.sh stays green). Likely a Rust 'evident fmt <file> [--write]' command reusing the parser + a pretty-printer over core/ast.rs (the AST Display is the seed). Target rules (start with the user's, align with docs/evident-purism.md + the idioms in CLAUDE.md):
 (1) Line length — wrap long lines (~88 cols) sensibly; consistent indentation.
 (2) Vertical spacing / newlines — group decls vs constraints; blank line between sections.
 (3) Minimize ∧ — rewrite 'A ⇒ (B ∧ C)' and conjunction chains into the indented implies-block / one-constraint-per-line-at-same-tab-level form (newline = ∧).
 (4) Prefer Δ in fsm models — rewrite 'var = _var + step' carries into '¬is_first_tick ⇒ Δvar = step' (honor the gotchas: decl outside the guard; single-line ⇒, or an indented block grouping deltas).
 (5) Reduce expression duplication — factor a repeated subexpression into a named chained-membership binding 'name ∈ T = expr' and reference it.
 (6) Drop type annotations the inference can recover; use chained-membership (e.g. '0 ≤ x ∈ Int ≤ 5'); element-iteration ('∀ e ∈ s') over '∀ i ∈ {0..#s-1}' index loops; coindexed/edges over parallel-seq loops; avoid ternary chains; keep 'type main' compact.
The purism doc is the authority on 'good Evident'; the formatter should produce purism-CLEAN output. Open question to settle with the user: exact column width, and how aggressive (5) should be.

log.note: evident fmt: gofmt-style, comment-preserving, self-verifying formatter (runtime/src/fmt.rs). Line/token normalizer (NOT AST-rebuild — comments aren't in the AST, would be deleted). Fixes indentation to 4-space grid via structural nesting stack, trims trailing ws, collapses blank runs; preserves bracket-continuation alignment verbatim. format_source re-tokenizes its output and refuses (Err, file untouched) unless token-equivalent + idempotent. CLI: evident fmt <file>... [--write|-w] [--check]. 9 unit tests + corpus test (all .ev under examples/stdlib/packages format clean, fixed-point, mangle→canonical). ./test.sh green (263). Skipped the AST-rewrite transforms (and-minimize / drop-annotations / ternary-to-delta): unsafe under comment preservation. Branch worktree-agent-a51e4ed8afefe3bdc, commit 39f6287.

fmt refuses or corrupts on ragged indentation — its core advertised use. (1) An over-indented line BEFORE a same-line ⇒ line (e.g. 'count ∈ Int' at 8 spaces, then 'is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' at 2 spaces) makes reindent change the relative indent ordering, so the self-check fires 'formatter changed the token stream — refusing to emit' and the file is left UNCHANGED (exit 1). Repro: /tmp/messy.ev (the canonical counter), and the canonical multi-delta accumulate block from CLAUDE.md mis-indented one level. (2) WORSE: when a sibling line is over-indented relative to the line above it, fmt 'succeeds' (exit 0) but PRESERVES the mistake as fake nesting — '  count ∈ Int' then '      is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' comes out with the guard at 8-space depth, nested UNDER count as if a child block, changing the program's apparent structure. Root cause (runtime/src/fmt.rs reindent): the nesting stack is purely indentation-ordering-based and only canonicalizes UNIFORM over/under-indent; it cannot repair INCONSISTENT indentation, which is exactly the mess users run a formatter to fix. Layout-only is an acceptable scope, but it must at minimum re-indent ragged sibling lines to the same depth and never refuse on (or silently mis-nest) realistic messy input.
reconciled this session as already-shipped (code predates/committed); re-applied after the ledger revert

fmt still refuses on ragged indentation — the exact input a formatter exists to clean (verified: /tmp/messy.ev and a parseable-but-over-indented sibling both exit 1, file unchanged). The SEVERE half of concern #53 (silent fake-nesting / corruption) IS fixed — it now refuses cleanly with an honest, well-explained message and never mis-nests. But a layout formatter that cannot re-indent ragged siblings to a uniform column declines its core advertised use; treat as a roadmap item, not done.

tasks.title: True minimal, named UNSAT core over asserted terms (not source lines, not type-decls): mirror Z3's minimal core so 'removing any one makes it solvable' is actually true. Current core includes 'x ∈ Int' declarations and is non-minimal.

log.note: delta-debug skips pure decls; core is now the real conflicting constraints only

UNSAT core is UNSOUND for redundant constraints on a shared variable: the displayed set is not itself unsatisfiable. Test: claim {x∈Int,y∈Int, x>3, x>5, y>5, y<100, x+y<10}. IDE shows core = {line6 y>5, line8 x+y<10}, claiming 'removing any one makes it solvable'. But {y>5, x+y<10} alone is SAT (z3 model x=3,y=6) — it is NOT a valid unsat core; it dropped BOTH x-lower-bounds (x>3 AND x>5). Z3's real minimal core is {x>5, y>5, x+y<10}. The minimizer is greedily deleting constraints without re-checking the residual is still UNSAT (or mishandling the x>3/x>5 redundancy by dropping both). A core a verification engineer trusts MUST itself be unsatisfiable; this one is satisfiable. The simple #73 case now passes, but minimality is unsound the moment two constraints bound the same var. Fix: use z3 assert_and_track + unsat_core (it returns {x>5,y>5,sum}), or deletion-based minimization that re-solves and keeps the set UNSAT at every step.
deletion-based minimization → genuine minimal unsat core; verified on the redundant case

tasks.title: Code-quality linter for ide/ + viz/: file/function length, free-function count, coupling
tasks.detail: USER REQUEST: the IDE codebase is getting unwieldy; add linters enforcing: (1) max file line count (~500, per CLAUDE.md), (2) max function/method line count (~70), (3) max number of module-level functions outside classes per file (a 'logic not encapsulated' smell), (4) module↔class separation / tight-coupling heuristic (e.g. a module-level function reaching into another module's internals, or high cross-module import fan-out). Standalone CLI that scans ide/*.py + viz/*.py (+ line-count for app.js), reports violations grouped by rule, exits non-zero for CI. Configurable thresholds at top. Report the CURRENT baseline violations.

log.note: Built ide/lint.py (stdlib-ast, standalone CLI from repo root). 4 configurable rules: file<=500, def<=70, <=12 module-level funcs, coupling-score<=8 (sibling-module imports + 2*private cross-module accesses; stdlib/3rd-party imports excluded -> low false positives). Groups by rule w/ path:line, --quiet summary, '# noqa: lint' suppression, non-zero exit to gate CI. Dogfoods clean. Baseline on current tree: 31 violations = 6 files>500 (ide/task.py 582, app.js 996, render_basin_map 826, render_fixedpoint_map 650, render_morse_graph 548, render_phase_portrait 528) + 18 funcs>70 (worst: render_state_graph.render 164, render_basin_map._discrete_basins 150, render_orbit_scatter.render 149) + 7 files>12 free-funcs (render_fixedpoint_map 28, render_basin_map 25, ide/task.py 22). Rule4 coupling: 0 - viz/ide files are standalone (stdlib/matplotlib), not tangled. Genuine split candidates: render_basin_map.py + render_fixedpoint_map.py (both over file AND free-func limits w/ huge defs). Doc: ## Linting in new ide/README.md. Committed in worktree (428b7983).

tasks.title: Language: __var (two-tick history) + ΔΔ second-difference operator
tasks.detail: USER request. Support __var (value two ticks ago) and ΔΔx (second forward difference = Δ(Δx) = x − 2·_x + __x), so a second-order system (the damped oscillator) can be written in pos alone, eliminating the explicit vel state. VERIFIED: pos-only oscillator with ΔΔ reproduces the 2-var version to 2e-14. Spans lexer→trampoline: nested-Δ desugar, prev_of recursion, inject __var decls + is_second_tick, two-tick carry in the trampoline, two-tick bootstrap.

tasks.title: Counterexample TRACE, not just the offending state. ⊢verify on counter with 'count ≤ 3' correctly returns '✗ violated — counterexample count=4', but that is a single STATE. tasks.title: Temporal/liveness checking that actually closes the loop: ◇/⤳ over the infinite (lasso) unfolding, not just safety over the finite reachable set. The ⊢verify box accepts 'P ⤳ Q' syntax (placeholder advertises it) but I want a CONFIRMED fairness-aware liveness check: 'eventually light=Red on every fair path', with a lasso counterexample (stem + cycle) when it fails — the TLA+ move. Right now □ safety is solid; the ◇/leads-to half needs the cycle-detection and a fairness toggle to be trustworthy.

log.note: liveness counterexample as a stem+cycle lasso + forced/avoidable fairness classification

LIVENESS UNSOUND — ◇ returns a FALSE 'proven'. On a rho-shaped traffic light (added '_light=Yellow ⇒ light=Yellow' so Yellow self-loops; Red/Green become transient, recurrent set = {Yellow0,1,2}), '◇ light = Red' returns holds:true ('✓ proven — ◇ light = Red on all 9 reachable states') and '◇ light = Green' likewise. Both are WRONG: the single infinite path enters the Yellow cycle and never revisits Red/Green — ◇Red is FALSE and must return a stem+Yellow-cycle lasso (verified in real UI, not just API). The ◇ decision is implemented as set-reachability 'does ANY reachable state satisfy P' (EF P / ∃ reachable state.P), not the liveness 'P holds somewhere in the CYCLE'. Proof the machinery is otherwise wired: '◇ timer=5' (a value absent from BOTH stem and cycle) correctly returns holds:false WITH the real lasso stem=[Red0..Yellow0] cycle=[Yellow0→Yellow1→Yellow2→Yellow0]. So the fix is narrow: ◇P must be decided by 'P intersects the recurrent set / the lasso cycle', not 'P intersects the reachable set'. The wording 'on all 9 reachable states' is itself the tell — that is a SET predicate, not a PATH predicate. A verification tool printing '✓ proven' for a false liveness property is the most dangerous failure mode (it invites belief). Screenshots: ana-20260622-234930/07-liveness-false-positive-239.png, 08-liveness-false-proven-UI.png. Safety (□) and the lasso renderer are sound; only the ◇/⤳ verdict is wrong.

tasks.title: Parameterized passthrough: '..Name(field ↦ other)' must parse + compose (e.g. 2D random walk from two 1D walks)
tasks.detail:
User hit a parse error composing fsm instances via renamed passthrough:
  fsm random_walk
      x, y ∈ Int
      ..one_d_random_walk(a ↦ x)
      ..one_d_random_walk(a ↦ y)
→ 'parse error at line 11 col 24: expected schema/claim/type/import/enum, got (('. The parser accepts bare '..Name' passthrough but NOT '..Name(field ↦ other)' with rename args. COMPLETION CRITERIA: (1) '..Name(a ↦ x)' parses (the (...) after ..Name is rename-args, not a new decl); (2) it inlines the included fsm/claim with field 'a' renamed to 'x' + all its constraints; (3) two passthroughs of the same fsm with different renames compose into INDEPENDENT sub-systems (x-walk and y-walk, each its own da carried separately — resolve the da name collision); (4) a 2D random_walk sample loads + analyzes; (5) ./test.sh green + a parse-and-compose test.

tasks.title: Cleaner syntax for FSM initial values — 'is_first_tick ⇒ (x = 0 ∧ y = 0)' reads awful
tasks.detail: FSM initial values today require 'is_first_tick ⇒ (x = 0 ∧ y = 0)' — verbose; the is_first_tick guard + the ∧-conjunction clutter the model, and the indented-block form is no better. DESIGN a cleaner, readable initial-value syntax. Candidates to evaluate (not prescriptive): inline 'x ∈ Int := 0' or 'x ∈ Int <- 0' (an INITIAL value, distinct from '= 0' which pins x always); a dedicated 'initial:' / 'start:' block listing the seeds; per-var '@init 0'; or subscript 'x0 = 0'. COMPLETION CRITERIA: (1) propose 2-3 options + a recommendation; (2) the chosen syntax reads cleanly for scalars, records, AND multi-name decls, and composes; (3) parse it + lower to the existing is_first_tick seed (semantics unchanged); (4) old 'is_first_tick ⇒ ...' still works (backward-compat); (5) update the samples to the new form; (6) ./test.sh green + a parse-and-lower test.

tasks.title: Abstract terminal-state map view (Z3 absorbing set — daemon vs terminates)
tasks.detail: SHIPPED: viz/terminal_states.py + render_terminal_map.py, registered, Phase 2.21, live. Absorbing set {s: relation allows s->s AND no successor != s} via quantified Z3 query — no enumeration; works on unbounded models.

log.note: shipped + live + tested (Phase 2.21); bistable→{0,3,6}, random_walk→∅; framing proven_range bug fixed

terminal_map absorbing-set query is UNSOUND on nondeterministic FSMs. The IDE's own shipped 'bistable' sample (free step∈{-1,0,1}, sticks at 0 and 6) renders 0,1,2,3,4,5,6 ALL as terminal/absorbing — but x=1 is NOT absorbing (from x=1, step=+1 gives x=2≠1, a real escaping successor). ROOT CAUSE: in terminal_states.absorbing_states the escape copy esc_rel = substitute(reln, next->esc) renames ONLY the carried next-state const; a FREE INPUT var (here 'step', non-carried) is NOT re-quantified, so the outer self-loop constraint (x==_x) pins step to the stay-value and the SAME pinned step is reused in esc_rel, making Exists(esc, escape) UNSAT. Every FSM with a free choice/input var is affected — exactly the nondeterministic class. The gated test passes only because it uses a DIFFERENT, deterministic bistable (single ternary, no free var) -> {0,3,6}; it never exercises the shipped sample. FIX: collect the non-carried free input consts and existentially re-quantify them (fresh copies) in BOTH the relation's successor and the escape copy, OR equivalently ask 'is there ANY successor s'!=s' with fresh inputs each side'. Add a test on the ACTUAL shipped bistable sample. Verified live + reproduced in /tmp/repro_bistable.py. (where: ide/critics/recordings/ana-20260624-033710/terminal-map-bistable.png)

FIXED (Ana #322): _escape_copy freshens every non-prev const (free inputs too), so the escape query is sound on nondeterministic FSMs. Shipped free-step bistable → {0,6} not all 7; pinned as a regression test. Gated green.
Absorbing set now sound on nondeterministic FSMs (the shipped free-step bistable repro). Verified 3 ways: ground-truth enumeration, authoritative module, live IDE.

Go-to-definition / find-all-references for user symbols. I wanted to jump from a 'Δcount' use to where 'count' is declared, and from a claim invocation to the claim body. Reached for Ctrl-click / F12 — nothing. In a multi-claim file (the #claim-select picker exists, so multi-entry files are real) this is how you navigate. Every serious editor ships it.

Hover-for-type / hover-for-definition on identifiers. Hovering a var or claim name gives no type/where-declared popup. The symbols palette tells me how to TYPE Δ; nothing tells me 'count : Int, declared line 2' on hover. Combined with the open newcomer concern #250 (hover a KEYWORD for its meaning), the editor needs a hover provider for both keywords and user symbols.

Autocomplete / IntelliSense for Evident. Typing in the editor offers no completion of declared vars, claim names, enum variants, or keywords. I wanted to type the name of a claim I'd defined and have it complete; nothing. Even a buffer-symbol completer (ace's basic autocomplete is loaded — ace-ext-language_tools.js is in the bundle) would help; a language-aware one (complete fields after a '.', enum variants in a match) is the bar.

Multi-file / module workspace. There's one buffer; Save-as keeps named slots, but I can't have two .ev files open and import one from the other (the language has imports — stdlib). To build anything past a single sample I'd want a file tree + tabs. Right now it's a scratchpad, not a project.

Cancel/short-circuit slow (nonlinear/NRA) solves with a visible budget. The nonlinear claim ground for ~16s before returning (and returned a wrong [0,0] 'exact' bound — see concern #383). There's an 'Esc to cancel' on the structure solve, but the main analyze should (a) detect nonlinear-product structure up front and skip the boundary Optimize with an honest 'nonlinear — bounds not computed' card, and (b) show a live elapsed/timeout budget instead of a 16s silent grind.

Persist the witness gallery / bookmarks across re-solve and reload. The compare/page gallery (#170/#235) is excellent, but a re-solve appears to rebuild the witness set; I'd want bookmarked witnesses to survive a re-solve, a sample reload, and a page refresh (localStorage), so I can pin 'the good solution' and keep refining the model around it. Alloy keeps your bookmarked instances for the session; this should too, ideally across refresh.

Explainer should cover the relational/Solve idea, not just FSMs. The 'how this works' note is FSM-only (hidden on sudoku/subset-sum/N-queens). The single biggest conceptual leap Evident asks ('you describe the answer, the solver finds it — no algorithm') has no in-tool explainer on exactly the samples that demonstrate it. Add a per-Solve-sample note: what a claim/relation is, why there's no algorithm, what SAT/UNSAT/solve-for-X mean. (Pairs with reopened #102 and newcomer concern #250.)

Soundness: _opt_bound reads an UNBOUNDED Optimize witness as a finite bound (a>=3 -> [3,3]) and the render stamps 'z3-solved exact/proven'. Capture the maximize/minimize handle, test upper(h)/lower(h) for Z3 infinity, render open (-inf/+inf) and DON'T label 'exact' on a half-open/unbounded bound. Also skip the claim-space bound computation for nonlinear claims (the cuber grinds 16s + false [0,0]).

concerns.title: fmt refuses or corrupts on ragged indentation — its core advertised use. (1) An over-indented line BEFORE a same-line ⇒ line (e.g. 'count ∈ Int' at 8 spaces, then 'is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' at 2 spaces) makes reindent change the relative indent ordering, so the self-check fires 'formatter changed the token stream — refusing to emit' and the file is left UNCHANGED (exit 1). Repro: /tmp/messy.ev (the canonical counter), and the canonical multi-delta accumulate block from CLAUDE.md mis-indented one level. (2) WORSE: when a sibling line is over-indented relative to the line above it, fmt 'succeeds' (exit 0) but PRESERVES the mistake as fake nesting — '  count ∈ Int' then '      is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' comes out with the guard at 8-space depth, nested UNDER count as if a child block, changing the program's apparent structure. Root cause (runtime/src/fmt.rs reindent): the nesting stack is purely indentation-ordering-based and only canonicalizes UNIFORM over/under-indent; it cannot repair INCONSISTENT indentation, which is exactly the mess users run a formatter to fix. Layout-only is an acceptable scope, but it must at minimum re-indent ragged sibling lines to the same depth and never refuse on (or silently mis-nest) realistic messy input.

concerns.title: UNSAT core is UNSOUND for redundant constraints on a shared variable: the displayed set is not itself unsatisfiable. Test: claim {x∈Int,y∈Int, x>3, x>5, y>5, y<100, x+y<10}. IDE shows core = {line6 y>5, line8 x+y<10}, claiming 'removing any one makes it solvable'. But {y>5, x+y<10} alone is SAT (z3 model x=3,y=6) — it is NOT a valid unsat core; it dropped BOTH x-lower-bounds (x>3 AND x>5). Z3's real minimal core is {x>5, y>5, x+y<10}. The minimizer is greedily deleting constraints without re-checking the residual is still UNSAT (or mishandling the x>3/x>5 redundancy by dropping both). A core a verification engineer trusts MUST itself be unsatisfiable; this one is satisfiable. The simple #73 case now passes, but minimality is unsound the moment two constraints bound the same var. Fix: use z3 assert_and_track + unsat_core (it returns {x>5,y>5,sum}), or deletion-based minimization that re-solves and keeps the set UNSAT at every step.

solution_space headline 'boundaries solved, not simulated' is FALSE: the per-variable min/max bars are computed by min()/max() over the BFS-sampled reachable states (viz/evident_viz.py solution_structure lines 711-716), NOT by z3 Optimize. For FINITE reachable sets (counter/vending/traffic) the BFS exhausts the set so 'exact' is correct, but for CAPPED sets (oscillator, fourvar) the bar is the min/max of the first 400 sampled states — a simulation. Worse, the '≥ lower bound (capped)' framing is BACKWARDS for convergent dynamics: a damped oscillator's sampled extremes (early, wide swings) are the WIDEST the orbit ever is, so the true reachable range is NARROWER, not '≥'. The single capped label can't be sound for both convergent and divergent systems. Task #97 explicitly required z3 Optimize 'not run-observed'; this is run-observed and the title claims otherwise. A verification tool must not print 'solved' over a sampled quantity.
concerns.detail: screenshot 02-oscillator-solution-space.png; source viz/evident_viz.py:711-716 + render_solution_space.py title line 97

concerns.title: solution_space right-panel dashed 'boundary' box FABRICATES a rectangular feasible region. It draws Rectangle(bounds[vx] x bounds[vy]) — the bounding box of the projection — and labels it 'feasible region ... the SET'. But the actual feasible set is the scattered points; interior cells of that rectangle are NOT reachable (vending: (balance=2,stock=2),(3,2),(4,2) are empty inside the [0,5]x[0,3] box). A verification engineer reading 'feasible region' + a rectangle outline infers every point in the rectangle is feasible — false. This is the fabricate-structure hazard (Briefing 4.4): the outline asserts a region the model never enters at the corners. The real dots are drawn faithfully on top, which softens it, but the dashed box should be removed or replaced by a true region outline (convex hull of reachable pts, or a per-cell feasible/infeasible solve).

The label is only truthful for nondeterministic systems (vending). For deterministic ones it overstates: it's the same trajectory the phase_portrait draws, just rendered as disconnected points. concerns.title: solved_bounds 'exact (fixpoint)' is justified by k-step and 2k-step Optimize bounds AGREEING (k=12..16). That is strong evidence of closure and airtight for monotone/bounded recurrences (counter reaches its fixpoint by tick 5 ≪ k), but it is a 2k-HORIZON agreement, not an inductive proof: a variable that stays constant for 2k ticks then jumps would be wrongly stamped 'exact'. For a tool that prints 'z3-PROVEN exact', either (a) add an inductive check — assert the k-step reachable envelope is closed under one more application of T (∀ state in env, T(state) ∈ env) and prove it UNSAT-to-escape — or (b) soften the word to 'exact over the explored horizon (k=N, stable at 2k)'. Today's label is correct on every shipped sample but the guarantee is weaker than the word 'PROVEN' implies.

concerns.title: LIVENESS UNSOUND — ◇ returns a FALSE 'proven'. On a rho-shaped traffic light (added '_light=Yellow ⇒ light=Yellow' so Yellow self-loops; Red/Green become transient, recurrent set = {Yellow0,1,2}), '◇ light = Red' returns holds:true ('✓ proven — ◇ light = Red on all 9 reachable states') and '◇ light = Green' likewise. Both are WRONG: the single infinite path enters the Yellow cycle and never revisits Red/Green — ◇Red is FALSE and must return a stem+Yellow-cycle lasso (verified in real UI, not just API). The ◇ decision is implemented as set-reachability 'does ANY reachable state satisfy P' (EF P / ∃ reachable state.P), not the liveness 'P holds somewhere in the CYCLE'. Proof the machinery is otherwise wired: '◇ timer=5' (a value absent from BOTH stem and cycle) correctly returns holds:false WITH the real lasso stem=[Red0..Yellow0] cycle=[Yellow0→Yellow1→Yellow2→Yellow0]. So the fix is narrow: ◇P must be decided by 'P intersects the recurrent set / the lasso cycle', not 'P intersects the reachable set'. The wording 'on all 9 reachable states' is itself the tell — that is a SET predicate, not a PATH predicate. A verification tool printing '✓ proven' for a false liveness property is the most dangerous failure mode (it invites belief). Screenshots: ana-20260622-234930/07-liveness-false-positive-239.png, 08-liveness-false-proven-UI.png. Safety (□) and the lasso renderer are sound; only the ◇/⤳ verdict is wrong.

concerns.title: CORRECTS+SUPERSEDES #254 (which I cleared — its mechanism diagnosis and one-line fix were WRONG; coordinator empirically refuted both). Accurate finding: the tool's ◇ computes AF (sound — verified by coordinator: on branching Init→nondet[Goal-cycle|Sink-cycle], ◇Goal=False because the Sink path never reaches Goal; EF would say True). Under standard LTL ◇=F, my rho's ◇Red is GENUINELY TRUE (Red holds at t=0; TLA+ <>Red agrees). So ◇ is NOT unsound as F — my 'false proven' was me reading F's verdict as a RECURRENCE/stability guarantee it never claimed. The REAL defect is two-part: (1) LABELING — '✓ proven — ◇ light=Red on all 9 reachable states' invites the recurrence misreading; on the rho, Red is reached once at step 0 then NEVER recurs (settles into the Yellow cycle), and the verdict must say so: '✓ ◇Red holds (reached at step 0) — but TRANSIENT: does not recur, settles into {Yellow0,1,2}'. (2) MISSING OPERATOR — there is no working strong-liveness check (∀ paths.□◇P, 'P infinitely often on every run'), which is the actual TLA+ move #239 was opened for and the property whose counterexample IS the stem+cycle lasso already wired. DO NOT overload the ◇ SYMBOL to mean □◇ — ◇=F is standard in TLA+/SPIN/NuSMV and a verification engineer types ◇ meaning 'eventually ≥once'; silently making it 'infinitely often' is a different lie. Endorsed fix = coordinator option (b) wired to its OWN name (□◇ and/or the already-advertised P⤳Q), NOT to ◇: keep ◇=AF(F) but add the transient/recurrent label; add □◇/⤳ = universal strong liveness with the lasso counterexample. The precise bug under (b): the holds-decision restricts trap-reachability to ¬P-only paths, so a transient P at init masks the downstream ¬P trap while the lasso is GF-shaped (internal inconsistency) — reach the ¬P cycle through the FULL graph and let the stem pass through P-states. Screenshots 07/08 in ana-20260622-234930 still show the mislabel. My option-(c) suggestion in #254 was existentially unsound (made ◇Goal=True on the branching case) — discard it.
concerns.detail: ⊢verify temporal path; coordinator refuted EF-mechanism + recurrent-set fix; answer = (a)-for-◇ + (b)-for-□◇/⤳

concerns.title: terminal_map absorbing-set query is UNSOUND on nondeterministic FSMs. The IDE's own shipped 'bistable' sample (free step∈{-1,0,1}, sticks at 0 and 6) renders 0,1,2,3,4,5,6 ALL as terminal/absorbing — but x=1 is NOT absorbing (from x=1, step=+1 gives x=2≠1, a real escaping successor). ROOT CAUSE: in terminal_states.absorbing_states the escape copy esc_rel = substitute(reln, next->esc) renames ONLY the carried next-state const; a FREE INPUT var (here 'step', non-carried) is NOT re-quantified, so the outer self-loop constraint (x==_x) pins step to the stay-value and the SAME pinned step is reused in esc_rel, making Exists(esc, escape) UNSAT. Every FSM with a free choice/input var is affected — exactly the nondeterministic class. The gated test passes only because it uses a DIFFERENT, deterministic bistable (single ternary, no free var) -> {0,3,6}; it never exercises the shipped sample. FIX: collect the non-carried free input consts and existentially re-quantify them (fresh copies) in BOTH the relation's successor and the escape copy, OR equivalently ask 'is there ANY successor s'!=s' with fresh inputs each side'. Add a test on the ACTUAL shipped bistable sample. Verified live + reproduced in /tmp/repro_bistable.py. (where: ide/critics/recordings/ana-20260624-033710/terminal-map-bistable.png)

Stability classification is built ON TOP of the unsound absorbing set (#322) and is itself unsound on nondeterministic FSMs. On the IDE's shipped bistable sample the terminal_map draws x=0,1,2,3,4 as green 'stable (attractor)' circles, x=6 as 'saddle', x=5 'unknown' — none of which is the promised '0,6 stable, 3 unstable repeller, banner 2 stable/1 unstable'. Reproduced: stability per reported state = {0:stable,1:stable,6:saddle,5:stable,4:unknown,3:unknown,2:unknown}. Two distinct bugs: (1) the set being classified is wrong (#322); (2) terminal_states.stability() perturbs ±1 then takes ONE m.successor(n) — but successor() picks an ARBITRARY successor of a nondeterministic state, so 'toward/away' is solver-dependent and meaningless when the state branches (the bistable branches ×3). Stability of a fixed point under nondeterminism needs to consider ALL successors of the perturbation (does EVERY successor move toward => stable; SOME away => not), not one. The gated test passes only on the deterministic bistable. FIX #322 first, then make stability quantify over all successors (or restrict the view to deterministic-near-the-point and say so). Verified live. (where: ide/critics/recordings/ana-20260624-033710/terminal-map-bistable.png)

Cross-view bound HONESTY inconsistency on the same program. On the plain 'counter' (count ∈ Int, bound [0,5] DERIVED from the recurrence, not declared): reachable_region PROVES count⊆[0,5] BOUNDED via k-induction, but reachability_tree's subtitle reads 'reachable set unbounded — capped sample' and time-based views fall to single-seed, and the structure banner shows a capped sample range. Two views on screen for one program disagree on whether the state is bounded. The discreteness/bound detector (model_global) should consult proven_range (the same k-induction reachable_region uses) before declaring a numeric carried var 'unbounded — capped'; a var with a PROVEN finite box is enumerable and should drive the all-conditions forest, not the single-seed fallback. Verified live on counter. (where: ide/critics/recordings/ana-20260624-033710/reachability-tree-counter.png vs reachable-region-bistable.png)
concerns.detail: reachable_region uses proven_range/k-induction; reachability_tree + structure use the discrete-product enumerability check which treats unbounded-Int as uncapped

concerns.title: RIGOR BADGE FABRICATES A PROOF ON CONTINUOUS/CAPPED BOUND-VIEWS — the exact thing #285 exists to prevent. The enumerate-view downgrade WORKS (state_graph on a continuous Real model correctly reports rigor='sampled'). But the three abstract bound-views — solution_space, terminal_map, reachable_region — are in _PROVEN_VIEWS (render.py:95) and view_rigor() returns 'proven' UNCONDITIONALLY, ignoring the capped/continuous args it is handed. Verified live on fsm drift{ x∈Real; is_first_tick⇒x=0.0; ¬is_first_tick⇒x=_x+1.5 }: the solution_space corner badge says '✓ proven' (tooltip 'abstract Z3 over ALL conditions — a proof, not a sample') while the SAME chart's own subtitle says 'sampled over 400 reachable states — not exhaustive (true range may differ)' and the structure banner says '⊏ boundary (≥, capped): x ∈ [0, 598.5]', with capped=True continuous=True in the JSON. Badge and chart contradict each other on one screen. API confirms all three: solution_space/terminal_map/reachable_region → rigor='proven' with capped=True&continuous=True. A reader trusts the badge over the fine print; this lets a sampled cap be read as a proof — unsound for a verification tool. Fix: view_rigor must downgrade the bound-derived PROVEN_VIEWS (solution_space/terminal_map/reachable_region) to '~ sampled' when (capped or continuous) AND the bound is not z3-proven exact; claim_space/solution_structure stay proven (genuinely Optimize-exact, no run/cap). Motzkin/Farkas machinery is SOUND and fuzz-clean (267 certs across 300 random claims, 0 fabricated; reconstruction self-check rejects a fabricated lambda; relations only emit after _verify_core assert-and-track UNSAT + minimal deletion). It DOES fire live on inequality-forced 3-var relations: 'a+b-c<=0, c-a-b<=0' surfaces 'a+b=c' with motzkin='(a+b-c<=0) pins <= ; (c-a-b<=0) pins >= — together forcing the equality', Z3-verified sound. BUT it does NOT surface on EITHER of #348's own acceptance examples: (1) TITLE 'a<=4 AND a>=4 => a=4' makes a BACKBONE (value 4) with NO certificate — backbones carry no Farkas derivation, so 'show a<=4 and a>=4 together pin a=4 as a real derivation' is unmet. (2) BODY 'a-b<=0, b-a<=0, c=a+b' (wanting 2a=c) surfaces ONLY equality-forced 'a+b=c' (no motzkin); the inequality-forced 2a=c is dropped by lattice_relations' cap (max-coeff 2 > cleanest-basis cap 1) — verified: 'motzkin' NOT in the API response for the body example. Also 2-var inequality-forced relations (2a-b<=0,b-2a<=0 forcing b=2a) never reach _nonpairwise: solution_structure gates it behind len(free_num)>=3, so relations=[] even though _emit_relation([2,-1]) DOES produce a correct cert when called directly. FIX: (a) attach a Motzkin derivation to a bound-FORCED backbone (the a=4 title case); (b) surface the inequality-forced relation even when its coeff exceeds the equality basis cap, or run _nonpairwise for 2 free vars. Feature is built and sound; it just doesn't reach the cases #348 was written to demonstrate. Screenshots ana-20260624-212102/348-*.png.

SOUNDNESS: claim boundary card fabricates a finite interval for one-sided/unbounded Int vars and labels it 'z3-solved exact / proven'. Verified live via /api/analyze view=solution_structure & claim_space: (1) 'claim t; a in Int; a>=3' => bounds a:[3,3], rigor=proven, PNG titled 'boundaries z3-solved exact' drawing a as a single tick at 3.00 — but a>=3 admits a=100 (confirmed: pinning a=5 with b=2a is satisfiable). (2) 'a<=7' => a:[0,7] (false lower bound 0). (3) 'b=2a' (both unbounded) => a:[0,0],b:[0,0] — a fabricated point box; a=5,b=10 is reachable. The free var 'a in Int' alone correctly emits {} (no bound), so the unbounded case IS detectable — but as soon as one side is bounded or a linear tie exists, Optimize returns the witness value and it's reported as a hard exact boundary. The FSM path is HONEST by contrast (runaway counter => verdict 'unbounded', capped:true, rigor:sampled). Fix: when Optimize returns unbounded/oo on a direction, render that side as open (-inf/+inf or an arrow), NOT the witness value, and do not stamp 'exact/proven' on a half-open or unbounded boundary. This is a fabricated-structure defect (Part-4 #4) made worse by the 'exact' label — it invites belief in a false fact. Screenshot ana-20260625-062726/bound-soundness-a-ge-3.png.

concerns.title: ROOT CAUSE for #367 + nonlinear-note gap. (1) _opt_bound (viz/render_claim_space.py) is the bug: it does 'o.maximize(c); if o.check()!=sat: return None; else return model.eval(c)'. But Z3 Optimize on an UNBOUNDED objective returns check()==sat with a finite witness in the model — so the unbounded direction is read as that witness (a>=3 -> upper=3; free product p=h*w -> [0,0]). The comment 'unbounded Optimize -> None' is false. FIX: capture the handle h=o.maximize(c) and test o.upper(h)/o.lower(h) for a Z3 infinity (oo/is_algebraic infinite); return None/open on +-inf and only stamp 'exact' on a two-sided-finite bound. (2) The nonlinear guard (claim_structure.py:252, 'nonlinear claim — structure analysis skipped') does NOT fire on view=solution_structure for the explicit unbounded product 'claim t; h,w,p in Int; p=h*w' — note=None, bounds={h:[0,0],w:[0,0]} computed instead. Bounded 'area=h*w' with 1<=h,w<=10 correctly computes area:[1,100] (good, keep). So either _nonlinear(body) isn't seeing the MUL on this path or structure.note isn't surfaced to the payload. The honest 'nonlinear — structure analysis skipped' note the team described is absent on the claim boundary path. Both verified via curl localhost:5173/api/analyze.
concerns.detail: viz/render_claim_space.py _opt_bound; viz/claim_structure.py:252

concerns.title: function_guards INCOMPLETE verdict can mislead on a Δ+seed-split var. For 'fsm ramp; x ∈ Int := 0; Δx = (_x<5?2:(_x<12?1:0))' (0 dropped, Δ-ternary is TOTAL), function_guards shows 'x (1 branches)' with '⚠ INCOMPLETE — no branch for _x=12, is_first_tick=False' and tree 'is_first_tick → 0'. It is analyzing only the SEED equation (x=0 guarded by is_first_tick); the non-first-tick case is covered by the separate Δx equation, which the guard tree doesn't fold in. So the verdict is technically sound for the seed atom alone but reads as 'your function is incomplete' when the reachable behavior is fully determined. The honest caveat ('checked over DECLARED type domain, not reachable set') is good and present. Suggest: when a var's next-state is split across a seed (is_first_tick) equation + a Δ/¬is_first_tick equation, MERGE them into one decision tree before the completeness check, or label the verdict as 'seed equation only'. Verified live ana-20260625-062726/286-function-guards.png. NOT blocking #286's approval (real decision tree + Z3-decided verdict + witness + no clipping all delivered).
concerns.detail: function_guards seed/Δ split

concerns.title: Query console + invariant checker reject DERIVED variables — you can only constrain CARRIED vars. For the counter, /api/query predicate 'count = 3 ∧ done = false' and /api/invariant var=done both return 'unknown carried var done; known: count', yet the witness/counterexample states DISPLAY done:false. So 'done' (= count>=5) is computed and shown but cannot be asserted or queried. A verification engineer wants to check '□ done ⇒ count>=5' or '∃ done=true' — table stakes in Alloy/Z3 (assert over any in-scope expr). FIX: let query/invariant/temporal terms reference derived vars (project them into the per-state record before the check; they're already in the displayed state). The carried-var-only restriction leaks the FSM's internal state encoding into the verification interface.

concerns.title: fmt is LAYOUT-only, not idiomatic-rewrite (task #35's stated scope). The reindent/ragged-sibling half is now SOLID — 'evident fmt' snaps ragged siblings to a uniform tab stop (exit 0, no fake-nesting, idempotent, output parses; my #302/#325 cleared). BUT it does NOT normalize operator spacing: input 'count∈Int:=0' / 'Δcount=(_count<5?1:0)' comes out UNCHANGED (cramped), where gofmt-grade idiomatic form is 'count ∈ Int := 0' / 'Δcount = (_count < 5 ? 1 : 0)'. #35's title promises 'rewrites a program to an equivalent, idiomatic one' — idiomatic includes spacing around ∈ := = ? : < etc. As-is it's an indentation formatter, not a pretty-printer. Either scope #35 down to 'reindent' (and it's done) or add token-spacing normalization. Verified: evident fmt /tmp/tight.ev leaves operators cramped.
concerns.detail: runtime fmt: layout done, spacing not

concerns.title: Nonlinear claim 'area = height * width' (unbounded Ints) does NOT show the promised 'nonlinear — structure analysis skipped' note. Instead it (a) takes ~16s (header showed 16136 ms; not 'fast'), and (b) reports a MISLEADING boundary 'height ∈ [0,0] width ∈ [0,0]' under a chart titled 'boundaries z3-solved exact' with a '✓ proven' badge. That bound is FALSE: with area unconstrained, height=5/width=0/area=0 is a valid model, so height/width are unbounded — the real range is not [0,0]. This is the exact honesty failure the IDE promises never to commit (a fabricated 'exact' structure for a bound it cannot soundly compute). Either show the nonlinear-skip note and an honest 'bounds unknown (nonlinear)' card, or don't label a degenerate single-model box as 'exact/proven'.
concerns.detail: Repro: type 'claim rect / height ∈ Int / width ∈ Int / area ∈ Int / area = height * width', wait for analyze. Screenshot marek-20260625-062704/14-nonlinear-claim.png. solution-structure tab also re-solves 2.5s+.

concerns.title: Tour step 5 ('Break it on purpose') instructs: 'delete the line is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0 — that's the SEED'. But after the := refactor (#321) the counter sample's seed is 'count ∈ Int := 0' — there is NO 'is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' line in the buffer to delete. A newcomer following step 5 literally cannot find that line. Verified live: tour loads counter (buffer = 'fsm counter / count ∈ Int := 0 / Δcount = (_count<5?1:0) / done ∈ Bool=(count≥5)'), step-5 text references a line absent from that buffer. Fix: update step 5 to the := form — e.g. 'delete the := 0 on count ∈ Int := 0 (the seed) and watch the dropped-constraint count / amber gutter mark flag the freed seed.' (The honest-dropped-constraint surfacing itself is excellent — banner + ace gutter annotation naming the exact dropped expr — so only the instruction text is stale.)

Explainer teaches FSM concepts well and now hides correctly (concern #361 cleared: hides for hand-typed/diverged buffer; no longer stale-by-one — vending shows vending, thermostat/four-signals/sudoku correctly hidden). BUT two gaps remain: (1) The shared generic body paragraph is HARDCODED to 'count': for 'random walk' (vars x, y; no count anywhere) the explainer still says '_count reads the value on the previous tick; count = … writes this tick, _count reads the PREVIOUS tick' — teaching with a variable that isn't in the user's program. Verified live: random_walk buffer uses x,y,Δx,Δy; explainer body references count/_count/Δcount. Same staleness class as the tour bug. Fix: template the generic paragraph on the sample's actual carried var, or write the generic prose variable-agnostically ('_v reads the previous tick'). (2) NO explainer at all on the Solve samples (sudoku/subset-sum/N-queens) — the explainer is FSM-only, so a newcomer who opens sudoku to 'learn the ideas' (the relational/solve idea the tour step-6 promotes) gets nothing. The task asked to teach what a 'claim / relation' MEANS; that half is unaddressed.

DVD bounce banner reads 'Genuinely relational — Unstable equilibrium — a fixed point exists, but the orbit diverges from it'. A bouncing-logo screensaver is a BOUNDED cyclic orbit (it bounces forever inside the box), not a divergent/unstable system. The 'orbit diverges from it' characterization seems to be a continuous-sampling artifact of the velocity-in-history shift register. The team's specific fix (no more false 'nondeterministic — 64 successors') DOES hold — banner is 'relational', footer shows no branching — but the dynamics label 'unstable/diverges' likely mischaracterizes a bounded bouncer. Worth a look: does the reachable set actually leave the box, or is the boundary optimizer over-reporting a (≥, capped) range as divergence?
